ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general kebab food product class; no specific veg seekh kebab entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for veg seekh kebab. Mapped to vegetable-based meat substitute cooked as closest defensible category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for veg seekh kebab; not a clinical substance requiring SNOMED mapping.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Veg Seekh Kebab is approximately 2 skewered pieces weighing around 100g, served as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Veg Seekh Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Veg Seekh Kebab (cr_id G00010785). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for similar paneer-and-vegetable kebab preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Seekh Kebab. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed-vegetable and legume patty/kebab preparations with paneer, which are predominantly protein and fat with low available carbohydrate (5.24g/100g). Reference: mixed vegetable dishes with chickpea/legume base typically GI 30–40 per University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 100g serving = (35 × 5.24) / 100 = 1.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080mg per 100g — far exceeds the 600mg/100g upper limit for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Veg Seekh Kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080mg/100g; if consumed, portion should not exceed 50g and preparation should use reduced salt and low-sodium spice blends.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low available carbohydrate (5.24g/100g) and low sugar (2.85g/100g) with moderate protein (10.7g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Veg Seekh Kebab is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate content and low GI (~35); however, the high fat content (26.4g/100g) warrants portion control, and pairing with a high-fibre salad rather than refined carbohydrates is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~7.2g/100g), trace trans fat (0.1g/100g), cholesterol (85mg/100g), and very high sodium (1080mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Veg Seekh Kebab due to its high fat, saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium profile; grilled preparation with minimal oil, reduced salt, and a small portion (≤80g) is advised if consumed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trace trans fat (0.1g/100g) is pro-inflammatory; sodium at 1080mg/100g exacerbates tissue swelling; zero dietary fibre reduces anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this preparation due to trace trans fat and very high sodium; if consumed, request grilled preparation with minimal oil, reduced salt, and note that green chillies are nightshade vegetables relevant to nightshade-sensitive RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Seekh Kebab is an Indian vegetarian dish consisting of minced mixed vegetables and housemade paneer (Indian cottage cheese) blended with aromatic herbs and spices such as cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and green chilli, shaped onto skewers and grilled or shallow-fried until golden. The texture is firm on the outside with a moist, spiced interior, and the flavour is savoury, mildly spiced, and herbaceous. It is commonly served as a starter, snack, or side dish in Indian restaurants across Singapore and South Asia, often accompanied by mint chutney and sliced onions. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7g protein, 26.4g fat, 5.24g carbohydrate, 2.85g sugar, and 1080mg sodium. The high fat content derives primarily from paneer and cooking oil. The very high sodium (1080mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients without significant modification. The low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index (~35) make it relatively suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion-controlled. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0g/100g. The dish contains dairy (paneer) and may contain gluten if breadcrumbs are used as a binder. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable.

How much sodium is in veg seekh kebab?
Is veg seekh kebab suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Veg Seekh Kebab (cr_id G00010785) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN analogous data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous mixed-vegetable and legume kebab category.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is a confirmed HPB-reported value, not an assumption — the preparation uses minimal fibrous vegetable content and the HPB record explicitly records 0.0g dietary fibre per 100g. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific veg seekh kebab preparation is not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026-04-16. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source for this dish. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB-reported panel is not available in the HPB Foodo source for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 5. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on input health_suitability notes referencing trace trans fat from frying oil; this is an estimated value and should be confirmed with laboratory analysis. 6. saturated_fat_g estimated at 7.2g/100g based on ICMR-NIN data for paneer-and-vegetable kebab analogues; not directly reported in HPB source. 7. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Veg Seekh Kebab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16.
